lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: What had he done, to make him fly the land?

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: You must have patience, madam.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: He had none:

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: His flight was madness: when our actions do not,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Our fears do make us traitors.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: You know not

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Whether it was his wisdom or his fear.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Wisdom! to leave his wife, to leave his babes,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: His mansion and his titles in a place

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: From whence himself does fly? He loves us not;

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: He wants the natural touch: for the poor wren,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: The most diminutive of birds, will fight,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Her young ones in her nest, against the owl.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: All is the fear and nothing is the love;

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: As little is the wisdom, where the flight

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: So runs against all reason.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: My dearest coz,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: I pray you, school yourself: but for your husband,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: He is noble, wise, judicious, and best knows

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: The fits o’ the season. I dare not speak much further;

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: But cruel are the times, when we are traitors

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: And do not know ourselves, when we hold rumour

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: From what we fear, yet know not what we fear,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: But float upon a wild and violent sea

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Each way and move. I take my leave of you:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Shall not be long but I’ll be here again:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Things at the worst will cease, or else climb upward

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: To what they were before. My pretty cousin,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Blessing upon you!

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Father’d he is, and yet he’s fatherless.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: I am so much a fool, should I stay longer,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: It would be my disgrace and your discomfort:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: I take my leave at once.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Sirrah, your father’s dead:

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: And what will you do now? How will you live?

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: As birds do, mother.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: What, with worms and flies?

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: With what I get, I mean; and so do they.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Poor bird! thou’ldst never fear the net nor lime,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: The pitfall nor the gin.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Why should I, mother? Poor birds they are not set for.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: My father is not dead, for all your saying.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Yes, he is dead: how wilt thou do for a father?

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Nay, how will you do for a husband?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Why, I can buy me twenty at any market.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Then you’ll buy ’em to sell again.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Thou speak’st with all thy wit; and yet, i’ faith,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: With wit enough for thee.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Was my father a traitor, mother?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Ay, that he was.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: What is a traitor?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Why, one that swears and lies.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: And be all traitors that do so?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Every one that does so is a traitor, and must be hanged.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: And must they all be hanged that swear and lie?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Every one.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Who must hang them?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Why, the honest men.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Then the liars and swearers are fools, for there are liars and

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: swearers enow to beat the honest men and hang up them.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Now, God help thee, poor monkey!

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: But how wilt thou do for a father?

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: If he were dead, you’d weep for him: if you would not,

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: it were a good sign that I should quickly have a new

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: father.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Poor prattler, how thou talk’st!

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Bless you, fair dame! I am not to you known,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Though in your state of honour I am perfect.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: I doubt some danger does approach you nearly:

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: If you will take a homely man’s advice,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Be not found here; hence, with your little ones.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: To fright you thus, methinks, I am too savage;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: To do worse to you were fell cruelty,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Which is too nigh your person. Heaven preserve you!

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: I dare abide no longer.

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Whither should I fly?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: I have done no harm. But I remember now

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: I am in this earthly world; where to do harm

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Is often laudable, to do good sometime

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Accounted dangerous folly: why then, alas,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Do I put up that womanly defence,

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: To say I have done no harm?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Where is your husband?

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: I hope, in no place so unsanctified

lines.speaker: LADY MACDUFF
lines.text: Where such as thou mayst find him.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: He’s a traitor.

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Thou liest, thou shag-hair’d villain!

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: What, you egg! Stabbing him.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Young fry of treachery!

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: He has kill’d me, mother:

lines.speaker: SON
lines.text: Run away, I pray you!

stage_directions.text: Enter Lady Macduff, her Son, and Ross.
Exit Lady Macduff, crying “Murder!” Exeunt Murderers, following her.

glosses.definition: address to a child or social inferior

glosses.phrase: Every one that does so is a traitor, and must be hanged
glosses.definition: the son's deadpan logic about swearing oaths — shocking innocence
deadly cruelty — the same 'fell' used by Lady Macbeth in 1.5
shaggy-haired cutthroat — the murderer attacks her son first

annotations.title: Lady Macduff's complaint
annotations.body:
Lady Macduff's speech is the play's most direct moral statement from a woman. 'He loves us not; / He wants the natural touch.' Her husband has left her and their children exposed, and she calls it a violation of natural obligation. Ross's excuse — that Macduff's 'wisdom' knows best — she rejects: 'Wisdom! to leave his wife, to leave his babes, / His mansion and his titles, in a place / From whence himself does fly?'

The scene presents a second feminine counterweight to Lady Macbeth. Where Lady Macbeth renounced the maternal, Lady Macduff inhabits it and insists on its claims. Her argument — that the abstract duty of revolt cannot excuse the concrete duty of protection — is not given a rebuttal by the play. Ross leaves, her son holds the moral stage with her for sixty lines, and then the murderers arrive.

annotations.title: The killing of the son
annotations.body:
The son is killed onstage: 'He has kill'd me, mother.' A thirteen-year-old Jacobean boy-actor speaks these words, and then dies while asking his mother to save herself. The scene is the play's most direct refutation of Lady Macbeth's 'I have given suck' boast from 1.7: here is the babe whose brains were dashed out, the promise kept. The image of the murdered child is now literal.

Shakespeare does not spare the audience. The scene runs to ninety-five lines, most of them the son's precocious back-and-forth with his mother ('What is a traitor?' 'Every one that does so is a traitor, and must be hanged'). The intimacy is deliberate. We watch the household for long enough to feel the domestic weight; then we watch it destroyed.

The scene has no strategic function in the plot. Its function is moral: to insist that the audience see what Macbeth's regime costs, counted in pennies.

trials.prompt: Lady Macduff's argument against Ross is:
options: Moral: abstract duty of revolt cannot excuse concrete duty of protection — her children come first

trials.prompt: The son's onstage murder is Shakespeare's:
options: Direct refutation of Lady Macbeth's 'I have given suck' boast in 1.7 — here is the murdered babe, made literal

trials.prompt: The scene's function in the plot is:
options: Moral — no strategic function; it forces the audience to see the human cost of Macbeth's regime
